old_claim.note: Printing Press (Movable Type) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No dependency remains because the paper-money node is scoped to late Tang merchant receipts and Song government paper money, not Gutenberg-style movable-type printing.

source_shape.source_locator: From Copper Coins to Paper Notes section describing late Tang merchant receipts, Song deposit-shop certificates, and government takeover in the 1020s.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source explains paper money as an outgrowth of paper receipts, deposit shops, and state monetary administration in China, with no dependence on movable-type printing presses.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The old edge conflated paper currency with later print-production technologies. The relevant prerequisites are paper as a medium and deposit/banking institutions, not a metal movable-type screw press.

ontology_before: Paper money depended on paper making, early banking, and movable-type printing.
ontology_after: Paper money depends on paper making as a material substrate and early banking/deposit practices as institutional predecessors.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: The graph no longer routes Chinese paper-money origins through Gutenberg-style movable-type printing.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to a later anti-counterfeit banknote printing process that explicitly used movable type.
A separate paper-money printing-security node were introduced with specific evidence for movable-type marks.
A source showed the first scoped paper money originated from movable-type print production rather than deposit receipts and state monetary administration.
short_reason: Early paper money emerged from paper receipts and deposit institutions, not movable-type printing presses.
